Opens with 'Existing approaches to roof segmentation for solar potential estimation divide into two families...'
Names DSM rasterization explicitly with downstream consequence: 'systematic orientation errors'
'annotating thousands of rooftops per city is prohibitive'
AHN3, Rotterdam/Amsterdam/Utrecht, 12,000 rooftops all named
Three contributions are substantively distinct, not cosmetically balanced
None of the flagged adjectives appear
Uses \citep{}, \begin{enumerate}, \, spacing — fully LaTeX-formatted
'In this paper, we address this gap by presenting...' — classic filler opener in second paragraph
Explicitly names 2D rasterization with explanation
Named as scalability bottleneck
AHN3, three cities, 12,000+ rooftops all present
Three-part (i)/(ii)/(iii) triad reads as formulaic filler structure
'robust' in contribution (ii), 'state-of-the-art' in paragraph one
Uses \cite{}, --- em-dash, ~ non-breaking spaces

notes: With updated discriminating assertions, the skill shows a clear +0.23 pass rate advantage (0.952 vs 0.720).

The most consistent discriminating signal is acronym expansion convention: the baseline writes 'AHN3 (expansion)' in both evals 0 and 1, getting the convention backwards. The skill gets it right both times ('expansion (AHN3)').

Sentence rhythm (no-symmetrical-sentence-structures) fails for BOTH configurations on the abstract eval. This is a genuine skill gap — the voice profile mentions rhythm variation but the abstract section guidance didn't enforce it. Fix applied: abstract section now explicitly requires at least one short sentence among longer ones.

Eval 2 (gap-and-contribution) remains the most discriminating: with_skill 7/7 vs without_skill 4/7. Baseline failures: filler opener, symmetrical triad, 'robust'/'state-of-the-art' as empty adjectives.
The skill costs ~9s and ~3500 tokens more on average. Given the manual revision effort these tasks would otherwise require, the overhead is negligible.
